hardware.cache_sizes: L1d: 1 MiB (32 instances), L1i: 1 MiB (32 instances), L2: 32 MiB (32 instances), L3: 44 MiB (2 instances)
hardware.simd_support: avx512f, avx512dq, avx512bw, avx512vl, avx2, sse4_1, sse4_2

lineitem.notes: Largest table by far, accessed by all queries. Date-filtered scans dominate. High parallelism opportunity.

orders.notes: Join bridge between customer and lineitem in Q3. Date filter on o_orderdate is selective.

customer.notes: Q3 filters on c_mktsegment (low cardinality ~5 values). Good candidate for dictionary encoding.

supplier.notes: Not accessed by sample queries but referenced in schema.

part.notes: Not accessed by sample queries but referenced in schema.

partsupp.notes: Many-to-many bridge between part and supplier. Not used in sample queries.

nation.notes: Small dimension table, not accessed by sample queries.

region.notes: Tiny dimension table, not accessed by sample queries.

joins.notes: Dimension-to-fact join. Filter on customer first to reduce orders scan.
Fact-to-fact join. Both sides have date filters. Build hash table on filtered orders.

filters.notes: Date threshold filter. ~90% of data retained. Zone maps can skip blocks.
1-year range filter on date. Highly selective (~1/7 of data). Critical for Q6 performance.
Narrow range [0.05, 0.07]. ~20% selectivity. Combines with date filter.
Date threshold filter. Filters lineitem after join.
Date threshold filter on orders. Push this before join with lineitem.
Equality filter on low-cardinality column (~5 distinct values). ~20% selectivity. Apply first in join chain.

aggregations.notes: Very low cardinality (3-4 groups). Combinable aggregates. Use thread-local partial aggregation + merge. Expression computation (discount, tax) can be vectorized.
Medium-high cardinality (one group per order). Hash aggregation with partitioning. Result sorted by revenue DESC, then limited to 10 rows.
Global aggregation (no GROUP BY). Trivial to parallelize: local sums + final reduce.

ordering.notes: Sort on GROUP BY keys. Only 3-4 rows after aggregation, trivial cost.
Top-K query (LIMIT 10). Use heap-based top-K instead of full sort. Partial sort per thread + merge.

optimization_opportunities: Q1: Massive parallelism opportunity on lineitem scan. 64 cores with morsel-driven execution can achieve 50x+ speedup over single-threaded. Morsel size ~10K rows to fit in L3 cache.
Q1: Thread-local hash aggregation with 4 groups is trivial to merge. Use SIMD for expression evaluation (discount/tax calculations).
Q1: Date filter l_shipdate <= '1998-09-02' is highly selective. Use zone maps (min/max per block) to skip entire blocks. Clustered/sorted storage on l_shipdate would maximize benefit.

Q3: Join ordering critical. Execute as: (1) Filter customer on c_mktsegment, (2) Hash join with orders (filtered on o_orderdate), (3) Hash join with lineitem (filtered on l_shipdate). Build hash tables on smaller sides (filtered customer, then filtered orders).
Q3: Push-down filters before joins: c_mktsegment filter reduces customer by ~80%, o_orderdate reduces orders significantly, l_shipdate further filters lineitem.
Q3: Top-K optimization: Use parallel heap-based selection instead of full sort + limit. Each thread maintains local top-10, then merge heaps.
Q3: Parallel hash join on both join steps. Partition by join key to avoid contention. 64 cores can parallelize build and probe phases.
Q6: Simplest query — pure parallel scan with 3 range filters. Expected 60x+ speedup with 64 cores. Morsel-driven scan with thread-local sum accumulators.
Q6: Combine filters efficiently: l_shipdate, l_discount, l_quantity can be evaluated in single pass. Use SIMD for parallel comparison of 4-8 rows at once.
Q6: Zone maps on l_shipdate can skip blocks outside [1994-01-01, 1995-01-01). If data is clustered by date, this skips most blocks.
Columnar storage: All queries touch only a subset of lineitem columns. Columnar layout reduces I/O by 5-10x (lineitem has 16 columns, queries touch 3-8).
String dictionary encoding: c_mktsegment, l_returnflag, l_linestatus are low-cardinality strings. Store as integers to speed up comparisons and reduce memory.
Persistent binary storage: HDD is slow (ROTA=1). Preprocess lineitem into columnar binary format with zone maps. Zero-copy mmap access eliminates parsing overhead.
Bloom filters for Q3 joins: Build Bloom filter on filtered customer.c_custkey to pre-filter orders before hash probe. Similar for orders.o_orderkey → lineitem.
Late materialization: For lineitem scans, process only keys (orderkey, date) through filters and joins, then materialize price/discount columns only for surviving rows.
AVX-512 SIMD: Hardware supports AVX-512. Use for filter evaluation (compare 8-16 values per instruction) and arithmetic (discount/tax expressions).
Cache-conscious hash tables: With 44 MiB L3 cache and 64 threads, size morsels to ~10-20K rows. Use compact hash tables (Robin Hood or Swiss tables) for aggregation.

parallelism_opportunities.rationale: Largest table, accessed by all 3 queries. Morsel-driven parallel scan across 64 cores can achieve near-linear speedup (50-60x). Critical for Q1 and Q6.
parallelism_opportunities.implementation: Morsel size 10K-20K rows (fit in L3 cache slice). Atomic counter for morsel assignment. Thread-local result buffers.

parallelism_opportunities.rationale: Q1 and Q6 aggregate over full lineitem scan. Thread-local hash tables (Q1) or accumulators (Q6), then merge. Minimal contention.
parallelism_opportunities.implementation: Local-then-merge pattern. Each thread aggregates independently, final merge is trivial (4 groups for Q1, single value for Q6).

parallelism_opportunities.rationale: Q3 scans orders with date filter. Parallel scan with push-down filter before join reduces volume.
parallelism_opportunities.implementation: Morsel-driven scan with date filter applied per morsel.

parallelism_opportunities.rationale: Q3 scans customer with mktsegment filter. Smaller table but still benefits from parallelism.
parallelism_opportunities.implementation: Morsel-driven scan with string equality filter.

parallelism_opportunities.rationale: Q3 hash join between orders and lineitem. Partition both sides by join key (o_orderkey / l_orderkey) to enable independent parallel join of partition pairs.
parallelism_opportunities.implementation: Radix-partitioned hash join. Build hash table per partition in parallel, probe in parallel. ~64-128 partitions to fit in cache.

parallelism_opportunities.rationale: Q3 hash join between filtered customer and orders. Smaller join but still parallelizable.
parallelism_opportunities.implementation: Build hash table on filtered customer (small), parallel probe from orders.

column_access_patterns.notes: Low-cardinality flags, always accessed together. Dictionary encode and store adjacent.
Frequently used together in revenue calculations. Store adjacent for cache locality.
Critical filter column in all queries. Cluster/sort data by l_shipdate to enable zone map pruning.
FK to orders. Hash join key.
PK of customer, FK in orders.

critical_path_analysis.rationale: Q3 involves two hash joins with large intermediates, aggregation over ~50K-100K groups, and top-K sorting. Much more complex than Q1 (single scan + trivial aggregation) and Q6 (pure scan).

execution_phases.phase: Hash join customer → orders (with date filter)
execution_phases.notes: Build hash table on filtered customer, probe with filtered orders.
Largest join. Intermediate from first join may be large. Partitioning critical.
Use heap-based selection, not full sort.

optimization_priority: 2. Optimize join ordering and filter push-down
3. Use partitioned hash joins to avoid memory contention
4. Use Bloom filters to reduce join probe cost
5. Columnar storage + zone maps to reduce I/O

summary: TPC-H sample workload (Q1, Q3, Q6) is heavily scan-dominated on the large fact table 'lineitem' (6M+ rows at SF1). All queries exhibit massive parallelism potential with 64 available cores. Q1 and Q6 are pure parallel scans with aggregation (expected 50-60x speedup). Q3 is more complex with two hash joins and medium-cardinality aggregation but still highly parallelizable (40-50x speedup). Key optimizations: (1) Morsel-driven thread parallelism for all scans/joins/aggregations, (2) columnar storage for lineitem to reduce I/O by 5-10x, (3) zone maps on l_shipdate for block pruning, (4) dictionary encoding for low-cardinality strings, (5) partitioned hash joins to avoid contention, (6) SIMD for filters and arithmetic, (7) Bloom filters for semi-join reduction in Q3. Hardware is well-suited with 64 cores, 44 MiB L3 cache, 376 GB RAM, and AVX-512 SIMD support. HDD storage (ROTA=1) makes persistent binary columnar format critical to avoid repeated parsing overhead.
